evals.prompt: Build a webforJ registration form with fields name (TextField), age (NumberField), and email (TextField), bound to a User bean. Validate name is non-empty and at least 3 characters, age is between 18 and 120, and email matches the standard email pattern. On submit, write the bean if valid; otherwise the form should highlight the invalid fields. Use Jakarta validation annotations on the bean.
evals.expected_output: The agent creates a `User` bean with `@NotEmpty`, `@Length(min=3, max=...)` (or `@Size`), `@Min(18)`, `@Max(120)`, and `@Pattern`/`@Email` constraints from `jakarta.validation.constraints` (with `@Length` from `org.hibernate.validator.constraints` if used). It creates a view holding a `BindingContext<User>` constructed via `BindingContext.of(this, User.class, true)` (or `new BindingContext<>(User.class, true)`) and binds the fields. The submit click handler calls `context.write(bean)` and branches on `result.isValid()`. The default reporter handles error display per-field automatically.
expectations: Defines a `User` bean with Jakarta annotations imported from `jakarta.validation.constraints.*` (NOT `javax.validation`). `@Length` may come from `org.hibernate.validator.constraints` if used.

LOAD-BEARING: Constructs the `BindingContext` with the Jakarta-validation flag set to `true`, either `BindingContext.of(this, User.class, true)` or `new BindingContext<>(User.class, true)`. Without this flag, the Jakarta annotations on the bean are silently ignored and validation passes for everything.

LOAD-BEARING: Every `context.bind(...)` chain ends with `.add()`. A chain without `.add()` configures the binding but never registers it, the form silently fails to bind. The check FAILS if any `bind()` call lacks a terminal `.add()`.
Submit handler calls `context.write(bean)` (NOT `context.read(bean)`, that goes the wrong direction) and inspects `result.isValid()` before persisting / proceeding

Bean-Validation implementation is on the classpath. The check FAILS if the agent goes out of its way to ADD `org.hibernate.validator:hibernate-validator` + `org.glassfish.expressly:expressly` to a Spring Boot project that already has `spring-boot-starter-validation` (or any Boot starter that pulls it transitively, e.g. `spring-boot-starter-web`), pinning explicit versions on top of Boot fights its managed-dependency versions. The check ALSO FAILS if the project is plain webforJ (no Spring starters) and the agent omits both libraries entirely, then `JakartaValidator` cannot start and the form crashes. Acceptable: agent recognizes Spring transitively brings them, OR adds both explicitly only when no Spring starter is present.
Does NOT manually call `field.getValue()` for each field on submit and copy values into the bean, the whole point of `BindingContext` is to do that for you
Does NOT register a custom reporter for every field unless asked. The default reporter shows inline errors on core webforJ fields automatically.
Does NOT mix `javax.validation.constraints` with `jakarta.validation.constraints`. Jakarta is the modern package.

evals.prompt: I have an existing webforJ form view with a TextField named `nameField` and a ComboBox named `power`. The bean property is `name` (matching `power`, but the text field name doesn't match). Use auto-binding instead of manual bind() calls.
evals.expected_output: The agent calls `BindingContext.of(this, Bean.class, ...)` and adds `@UseProperty("name")` to the `nameField` declaration so the framework binds it to the `name` property. The `power` field auto-binds because its name matches the bean property.
expectations: Calls `BindingContext.of(this, Bean.class, ...)`, NOT `new BindingContext<>(Bean.class)` followed by manual `context.bind(...)` calls (the prompt explicitly asks for auto-binding)

LOAD-BEARING: Annotates `nameField` with `@UseProperty("name")` from `com.webforj.data.binding.annotation.UseProperty`. Without this, auto-bind tries to find a bean property called `nameField` and silently produces no binding.
Does NOT also manually call `context.bind(nameField, "name").add()` after calling `BindingContext.of(this, ...)`, that registers the binding twice
Does NOT add `@UseProperty("power")` to the `power` field, it already matches by name; redundant annotation is wrong style
Imports the annotation from `com.webforj.data.binding.annotation`, NOT from any other package

evals.prompt: I want my webforJ form to keep the Submit button disabled until the form is fully valid, and write changes to the bean as the user types. Show me the snippet.
evals.expected_output: The agent calls `context.observe(bean)` (which does an initial read then writes UI -> bean on every value change) and registers `context.onValidate(e -> submit.setEnabled(e.isValid()))`. The `onClick` handler still calls `context.validate()` (or relies on the bean already being current) before persisting.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Calls `context.observe(bean)`, NOT `context.read(bean)` alone. `read` does a one-time bean -> UI sync; without `observe`, value-change listeners are not registered and the bean is not auto-updated as the user types.

LOAD-BEARING: Uses `context.onValidate(e -> submit.setEnabled(e.isValid()))` (or the equivalent `context.addValidateListener(...)`) to drive submit-enabled state. Without this listener, the submit button stays in whatever initial enabled state was set, regardless of validity.
Sets the initial submit state to disabled (e.g. `submit.setEnabled(false)`) so the button is correctly off when the form is first rendered with empty/invalid values
Does NOT poll validity in a timer or check it inside `onClick` only, the documented pattern is event-driven via `onValidate`
Mentions or implies the unidirectional caveat: `observe` syncs UI -> bean, but later changes to the bean don't reflect back. If the bean changes externally, call `context.read(bean)` again.

evals.prompt: Add a phone number field to my webforJ form that formats input as `(XXX) XXX-XXXX` while the user types. Bind it to the `phone` property of my Contact bean.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses `MaskedTextField` (NOT a plain `TextField` with custom JS) with `setMask("(000) 000-0000")`. The field is bound via `context.bind(phoneField, "phone").add()`. The bean property is `String phone`.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Uses `MaskedTextField` from `com.webforj.component.field.MaskedTextField`, NOT a plain `TextField` with manual formatting, NOT a regex-only `setPattern(...)` (which only validates, doesn't format-as-you-type)

LOAD-BEARING: The mask is `"(000) 000-0000"`, the `0` token means 'any digit'. The check FAILS if the agent uses a non-existent mask token like `9` (jQuery-mask-style), `\d`, or attempts to use a regex
Imports from `com.webforj.component.field.MaskedTextField`
Binds with `context.bind(phoneField, "phone").add()` (or auto-bind with field name matching), the `String` value of the field maps directly to the bean's `String phone` property
Does NOT add a `setPattern(...)` call on the field that re-validates the same shape the mask enforces, the mask already guarantees the structure

LOAD-BEARING: Does NOT add a `@Pattern` (or any other regex-shape constraint) on the bean's `phone` property that re-validates the `(NNN) NNN-NNNN` shape. The mask already prevents any other shape from reaching the bean. Adding this is dead code that wastes attention and confuses the contract. Acceptable Jakarta annotations on `phone` are constraints the mask CANNOT express, like `@NotEmpty` (mask still lets the user submit empty) or `@Size(min=14, max=14)` (mask allows partially-filled input). Anything that re-asserts the digit-and-paren-and-dash structure FAILS this check.

evals.prompt: I have a webforJ Table with a `price` column showing values like `1234567.89` raw. Format it as a US currency string `$1,234,567.89` in the column.
evals.expected_output: The agent attaches a `CurrencyRenderer<>(Locale.US)` (or equivalent: a `MaskedNumberRenderer<>("$#,###,##0.00", Locale.US)`) to the column with `column.setRenderer(...)`. It does NOT use `MaskDecorator` inside a value provider or cell renderer because Table has dedicated mask renderers for this case. The renderer runs in the browser per cell, the server only ships the raw `Double`.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Uses a Table renderer wired with `column.setRenderer(...)`. Acceptable: `new CurrencyRenderer<>(Locale.US)` from `com.webforj.component.table.renderer.CurrencyRenderer`, OR `new MaskedNumberRenderer<>("$#,###,##0.00", Locale.US)` from `com.webforj.component.table.renderer.MaskedNumberRenderer`. The check FAILS if the agent uses `MaskDecorator.forNumber(...)` for this Table column, that is the wrong tool for Table cells.
Imports the renderer from `com.webforj.component.table.renderer.*`, NOT from `com.webforj.component.field` (that package is for INPUT components, not renderers)

Wires the renderer with `column.setRenderer(renderer)` after `table.addColumn("price", Product::getPrice)` (or chained with the column reference). The check FAILS if the agent calls `setRenderer(...)` on the table itself rather than on a column.

If `MaskedNumberRenderer` is chosen, the mask uses webforJ tokens: `0` (always digit), `#` (suppress leading zeros), `,` (grouping), `.` (decimal), `$` (literal). Wrong masks: Java `DecimalFormat` patterns, `String.format` patterns, `"\u00A4#,##0.00"` (currency symbol token does not exist on these renderers).

Does NOT call `MaskDecorator` anywhere, the prompt is about a Table column and the documented tool is a Table renderer. Suggesting `MaskDecorator` here is the wrong recommendation that the user explicitly flagged.
Does NOT instantiate `MaskedNumberField` (a UI input component) to format a Table cell, fields are for user typing, not for rendering

evals.prompt: I generate a daily PDF summary email outside any UI. I have a `LocalDate reportDate` and a `double totalRevenue`. Format them as `Report for July 04, 2025: $1,234,567.89` for the email body. The values are NOT in a Table, NOT in a form field.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses `MaskDecorator.forDate(reportDate, "%Ml %Dz, %Yl")` and `MaskDecorator.forNumber(totalRevenue, "$#,###,##0.00")` to compose the line. Both calls null-check the result before concatenating, since `MaskDecorator` returns `null` on invalid input. This is the documented use case for `MaskDecorator`: a non-input, non-Table surface where the mask syntax is wanted.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Uses `MaskDecorator` from `com.webforj.MaskDecorator` (top-level package, NOT under `component.field`, NOT under `component.table.renderer`). The check FAILS if the agent invents a different package.

Calls `MaskDecorator.forDate(reportDate, mask)` for the date and `MaskDecorator.forNumber(totalRevenue, mask)` for the number. The date mask uses `%` tokens (e.g. `"%Ml %Dz, %Yl"`, `"%Mz/%Dz/%Yl"`); the number mask uses `0`, `#`, `,`, `.`, `$` (e.g. `"$#,###,##0.00"`). Java `DateTimeFormatter` patterns or `DecimalFormat` patterns are wrong on these calls.

Null-checks the result of each `MaskDecorator` call before concatenating. `MaskDecorator` returns `null` on invalid input, and an unchecked `null` interpolated into a string produces literal `null` in the output.
Does NOT use a `Table` renderer (`CurrencyRenderer`, `MaskedNumberRenderer`, `MaskedDateTimeRenderer`), there is no Table involved
Does NOT use `MaskedTextField` / `MaskedNumberField` / `MaskedDateField`, those are UI input components, not stateless formatters
Does NOT roll a custom `DateTimeFormatter` / `NumberFormat` for this, the prompt is about webforJ formatting and `MaskDecorator` is the documented tool for non-input, non-Table surfaces

evals.prompt: Build a date-range picker in my webforJ form: a start date and an end date. Validation: start must be before end, end must be after start. When the user changes one, re-validate the other so cross-field errors update immediately.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses two `MaskedDateField` (or plain `DateField`) bound to `startDate` and `endDate`, each with `useValidator(...)` calls that compare against the peer date stored in the view. After each value change, the agent calls `context.getBinding("<peer>").validate()` to revalidate the other field.
expectations: Uses `MaskedDateField` (or `DateField`) from `com.webforj.component.field` for both start and end. Bound to bean properties of type `LocalDate` (or `LocalDateTime` if datetime).

LOAD-BEARING: Each binding has a `.useValidator(value -> peer != null && value.isBefore/isAfter(peer), "...")` predicate that checks against a captured peer value held in the view (a `LocalDate`/`LocalDateTime` field on the class)

LOAD-BEARING: After `start` changes, the value-change listener calls `context.getBinding("endDate").validate()` (and symmetrically for `end`). Without this manual revalidation, only the field the user just edited gets re-checked, and the peer's existing error stays stale.
Captures the latest peer value BEFORE calling `getBinding("...").validate()`, the predicate reads the captured peer field, so it must be assigned first in the listener
Does NOT try to register a single 'cross-field' validator outside of any binding, the documented pattern is per-binding validators that read external state
Does NOT skip the `.add()` at the end of each binding chain

evals.prompt: I have a webforJ form with First Name, Last Name, Email, Password, Confirm Password, Address, State, Zip, Submit, Cancel. Lay them out so on desktop they form a 2-column grid with Email and Address spanning both columns, but on mobile they all stack to a single column.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses `ColumnsLayout` with breakpoints: 1 column at narrow width and 2 columns at a desktop-class width (e.g. `40em` or `600px`). Calls `setSpan(email, 2)` and `setSpan(address, 2)`. Field declaration order in the constructor matches the visual flow.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Uses `ColumnsLayout` from `com.webforj.component.layout.columnslayout.ColumnsLayout`. `Breakpoint` and `Alignment` are referenced as `ColumnsLayout.Breakpoint` and `ColumnsLayout.Alignment` (static inner classes). The check FAILS if the agent treats them as top-level classes or imports them from a different package.
Sets at least two breakpoints: one at width `0` with `1` column (mobile fallback) and one at a desktop-class width (e.g. `600`, `"40em"`, `"48em"`) with `2` columns

LOAD-BEARING: Calls `layout.setSpan(email, 2)` and `layout.setSpan(address, 2)` so those fields take both columns on desktop. Without these calls, email and address would be paired with the next field rather than running full-width.
Field order in the layout constructor matches the requested visual flow: firstName | lastName, email (full), password | passwordConfirm, address (full), state | zip, cancel | submit (or similar)
Does NOT use `FlexLayout` alone, `FlexLayout` does not provide responsive breakpoints with auto-collapsing columns
Does NOT hand-roll CSS media queries for responsive behavior when `ColumnsLayout.Breakpoint` is the documented mechanism

evals.prompt: I want my webforJ MaskedDateField to ONLY allow date selection through the calendar picker, no manual typing. Set it up.
evals.expected_output: The agent calls `dateField.setAllowCustomValue(false)` to disable manual text entry and `dateField.getPicker().setAutoOpen(true)` so the calendar opens automatically when the field gains focus. This is the documented recipe for picker-only entry.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Calls `dateField.setAllowCustomValue(false)`, this is the call that disables manual typing. Without it, users can still type values that bypass the picker.

LOAD-BEARING: Calls `dateField.getPicker().setAutoOpen(true)` (or similar, the picker must be set to auto-open so the user has a way to actually pick a date). The combination of `allowCustomValue=false` AND `autoOpen=true` is the documented picker-only recipe.
Acceptable additional touches: `dateField.getPicker().setIconVisible(true)` so the calendar icon is visible; `dateField.setMask("%Mz/%Dz/%Yz")` (or another date mask) so the format is consistent
Does NOT recommend disabling the field entirely with `setEnabled(false)`, that blocks ALL interaction including the picker
Does NOT attempt to attach a key listener that swallows keystrokes, that's a fragile workaround when `setAllowCustomValue(false)` is the supported API

evals.prompt: My webforJ form has TextField nameField bound to a User bean's `name` property. The user wants password strength validation: at least 8 chars, at least one digit, at least one uppercase letter. Implement a reusable PasswordStrengthValidator.
evals.expected_output: The agent implements a class implementing `Validator<String>` from `com.webforj.data.validation.server.validator.Validator`, with a `validate(String value)` method returning `ValidationResult.valid()` or `ValidationResult.invalid(...)`. The validator is applied via `.useValidator(new PasswordStrengthValidator())` on the password binding (or via `@UseValidator(PasswordStrengthValidator.class)`).
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Defines a class implementing `Validator<String>` from `com.webforj.data.validation.server.validator.Validator`. The check FAILS if the agent uses a Spring `Validator`, a Jakarta `ConstraintValidator`, or rolls a `Predicate<String>` without going through the `Validator` interface (the prompt asks for a reusable validator class).

LOAD-BEARING: The `validate(...)` method returns `ValidationResult.valid()` or `ValidationResult.invalid("message")` from `com.webforj.data.validation.server.ValidationResult`. The check FAILS if the agent returns `null`, `boolean`, or a custom result type.

The validator checks all three constraints: length >= 8, at least one digit, at least one uppercase letter. Returning `invalid("...")` on the first failure is fine; aggregating all failure messages is also fine.

Applies the validator with `.useValidator(new PasswordStrengthValidator())` (or `@UseValidator(PasswordStrengthValidator.class)` on the field), NOT `.useValidator(value -> ..., "message")` (that's an inline predicate, not a reusable validator class)
Does NOT use Jakarta `@Pattern` for this, the prompt asks for a reusable validator class. Jakarta `@Pattern` would be a different (also valid) approach, but not the requested one.

evals.prompt: My Order bean has property `orderDate` of type `String` in `yyyy-MM-dd` format, but I want to bind it to a `MaskedDateField` (which works in `LocalDate`). Set up the binding correctly.
evals.expected_output: The agent writes a `Transformer<LocalDate, String>` that uses a `DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd")` to convert between component `LocalDate` and bean `String`. The binding declares the bean type as the third arg to `bind`: `context.bind(dateField, "orderDate", String.class).useTransformer(new DateTransformer()).add();` so the framework knows the bean uses `String` while the component uses `LocalDate`.
expectations: LOAD-BEARING: Implements `Transformer<LocalDate, String>` from `com.webforj.data.transformation.transformer.Transformer`. The generic order is component-type FIRST, then model-type. The check FAILS if the agent reverses the generics (`Transformer<String, LocalDate>`).
Implements both `transformToComponent(String modelValue)` returning `LocalDate` and `transformToModel(LocalDate componentValue)` returning `String`
Uses `java.time.format.DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd")` to format/parse. NOT webforJ mask syntax (`%Yl-%Mz-%Dz`), that's for the field's display mask, not for converting model strings.

LOAD-BEARING: When binding, passes the bean type as a THIRD argument to `bind`: `context.bind(dateField, "orderDate", String.class)`. Without this, the framework assumes the bean type matches the component type and the transformer is misapplied.
Calls `.useTransformer(new DateTransformer())` (or `@UseTransformer(DateTransformer.class)` on the field) on the binding builder
Throws `TransformationException` (from `com.webforj.data.transformation`) on parse failure, not a generic `RuntimeException` and not silently returning `null`
